questions.question: Explain the Sequential Access Method in file systems. How does the read operation work in this method?
questions.hint: Think about how data is read from beginning to end without jumping
keyPoints: Sequential access reads files record by record in order
Uses a read pointer that moves forward through the file
Each read operation advances the pointer by one record
Cannot skip or jump directly to a specific record
Simple to implement and efficient for batch processing
Commonly used in magnetic tape systems

questions.question: What is Direct Access (Random Access) method? Explain how it differs from sequential access.
questions.hint: Compare with sequential access - think about direct addressing
keyPoints: Direct access allows reading records in any order without
Uses block number or record number to access directly
No need to read preceding records
Uses seek operation to position the read/write head
Suitable for database systems and interactive applications
More complex hardware support required (magnetic disks)

questions.question: Define Indexed Access Method. What are the components of an index in file systems?
questions.hint: Think of a book index - it points to locations, not the content itself
keyPoints: Indexed access uses an index file to locate records
Index contains pointers to actual data blocks
Two-level or multi-level indexing improves efficiency
Components: Index file, Data file, Primary key
Reduces search time from O(n) to O(log n) for indexed field
Requires additional storage for index

questions.question: Compare Sequential Access and Direct Access methods in terms of speed, storage, and applications.
questions.hint: Make a table comparing the two methods
keyPoints: Sequential: slower for random access, simpler, cheaper storage
Direct: faster for random access, requires seek mechanism
Sequential used in: batch processing, tape systems, log files
Direct used in: databases, transaction processing, interactive systems
Sequential has sequential I/O overhead, direct has random I/O overhead
Direct access requires more sophisticated hardware

questions.question: What is the Relative Record Number (RRN) in direct access files? How is the address calculated?
questions.hint: Remember to account for starting position (0 or 1) in calculation
keyPoints: RRN is the logical position number of a record in a file
Starts from 0 or 1 depending on system
Physical address = (RRN - 1) × Record size
File is treated as an array of fixed-size records
Also called Relative File Organization
Provides mapping between logical and physical locations

questions.question: Explain the concept of File Allocation Methods. How do they relate to access methods?
questions.hint: Remember: Indexed allocation best supports both access methods
keyPoints: Three methods: Contiguous, Linked, and Indexed allocation
Contiguous allocation: consecutive blocks - good for sequential, poor for direct
Linked allocation: scattered blocks linked by pointers - good for sequential only
Indexed allocation: uses index block - supports both sequential and direct
Allocation method directly impacts access efficiency
Choice depends on type of access required (sequential/direct)

questions.question: With a neat diagram, explain the working of an Indexed File Organization. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages.
questions.hint: Draw diagram showing relationship between index and data blocks
keyPoints: Contains: Data file, Index file (one or more levels)
Index file has entries: key value + pointer to data block
Two-level index: Primary index points to secondary indices
Search process: Read index → Find block pointer → Read data block
Advantages: Fast random access, efficient for range queries, scalable
Disadvantages: Extra storage for index, overhead for index maintenance, slower sequential access

questions.question: Explain the three main file access methods supported by operating systems. Compare them with respect to data retrieval speed and suitable applications.
questions.hint: Cover all three methods with comparison table
keyPoints: Sequential Access: Records accessed in order, read pointer advances
Direct Access: Records accessed by address/position, no order required
Indexed Access: Uses index for direct lookup, combines with sequential/direct
Sequential speed: Fast for sequential, very slow for random access
Direct speed: Fast for random access, moderate for sequential
Indexed speed: Fastest for both with proper indexing
Applications: Sequential - batch processing; Direct - databases; Indexed - large databases with search

questions.question: Explain how file access methods are implemented in operating systems. Discuss the role of system calls like open, read, write, and seek.
questions.hint: Connect system calls to access methods they support
keyPoints: open: Establishes connection, returns file descriptor, sets file pointer
read: Transfers data from file to buffer, advances file pointer
write: Transfers data from buffer to file, advances file pointer
seek: Repositions file pointer for random access
For sequential: read/write automatically advance pointer
For direct: seek used to position, then read/write
OS maintains File Control Block (FCB) with file position, mode, etc.

questions.question: A file contains 1000 fixed-length records. Each record is 80 bytes long and the block size is 512 bytes. Calculate: (a) Number of records per block (b) Number of blocks required (c) Average number of block accesses for sequential access and direct access to fetch a record.
questions.hint: Remember to use ceiling function when records don't divide evenly
keyPoints: (a) Records per block = 512 / 80 = 6 records per block (ignore remainder)
(b) Number of blocks = ceil(1000 / 6) = 167 blocks
(c) Sequential access: Average = n/2 = 1000/2 = 500 record accesses = 500/6 ≈ 83 block accesses
(c) Direct access: Average = 1 block access (using RRN)
For sequential worst case: 167 block accesses
For direct worst case: 1 block access

questions.question: Discuss the advantages and limitations of Sequential Access Method. Under what circumstances would you choose sequential access over direct or indexed access?
questions.hint: Think about use cases like log files, backup tapes, report generation
keyPoints: Advantages: Simple implementation, low storage overhead, reliable, good for batch processing, efficient for full file scans
Limitations: Slow random access, cannot skip records, not suitable for interactive applications
Choose sequential when: Processing entire file, sequential workloads dominate, simple and reliable storage (tapes), limited random access needs
Not suitable when: Quick access to specific records needed, multiple users accessing randomly, real-time requirements

questions.question: A sequential file has 5000 records. Each record is 100 bytes and block size is 2000 bytes. Calculate: (i) Blocking factor (ii) Number of blocks (iii) Maximum and minimum block accesses to find a record.
questions.hint: Use floor for blocking factor, ceiling for number of blocks
keyPoints: Blocking factor = floor(2000/100) = 20 records per block
Number of blocks = ceil(5000/20) = 250 blocks
Minimum block accesses: 1 (record in first block)
Maximum block accesses: 250 (record in last block)
Average block accesses for successful search: (250+1)/2 ≈ 125.5
Unsuccessful search: 250 block accesses

questions.question: In a direct access file using Relative Record Number (RRN), the record size is 128 bytes and the disk block size is 4096 bytes. If the file contains 10000 records, calculate: (a) The physical address of record with RRN=5678 (b) How many records can be stored per block (c) Number of disk blocks needed.
questions.hint: For RRN starting from 1: byte offset = (RRN-1) × record size
keyPoints: (b) Records per block = floor(4096/128) = 32 records
(c) Blocks needed = ceil(10000/32) = 313 blocks
Physical address includes block number and offset within block
Block number = 726656 / 4096 ≈ 177
Offset within block = 726656 % 4096 = 1952 bytes

questions.question: Explain the concept of Dynamic File Access. How do modern operating systems support multiple access methods simultaneously? Discuss with examples.
questions.hint: Think about how same file can be accessed both sequentially and randomly
keyPoints: Dynamic access allows mixing sequential and direct operations
Modern OS supports: Sequential (read sequentially), Direct (seek+read), Indexed (via database systems)
File Control Block (FCB) maintains current position for sequential access
seek system call enables direct access by repositioning
Example: UNIX supports both through read, write, lseek
Database systems implement indexed access on top of OS file calls
Operating system provides primitives; applications choose method
